15: reeh natati lefanecha hayom et-hahayyim veet-hatov veet-hamot veet-hara (rĕʾēh nātattî lĕpānêkā hayyôm ʾet-hahayyîm wĕʾet-haṭṭôb wĕʾet-hammāwet wĕʾet-hārāʿ): 'See I have set before you today life and good, and death and evil.' The covenant's binary choice — life or death, blessing or curse — is Israel's definitive moral situation. Paul's Christological reading of Deut 30 in Romans 10:6-8 is one of his most daring hermeneutical moves: the Torah's own accessibility-language ('not up in heaven, not across the sea, but very near you') is applied to the word of Christ — the gospel is the Torah's own principle of accessibility now embodied in the proclaimed word of faith.
1: The conditional opener — כִּי יָבֹאוּ עָלֶיךָ כָּל הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (kî yābōʾû ʿālêkā kol-haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh) — "when all these things come upon you, the blessing and the curse" — assumes that both outcomes will be experienced. The construction is not a simple conditional ("if") but anticipatory ("when"). Moses does not present the curse as merely hypothetical; he projects a future that includes exile. This verse opens the covenant's most forward-looking section: what happens after catastrophe.
2: וְשַׁבְתָּ (wĕshavtā) — "and you return": the verb shûb (H7725) is Deuteronomy's signature term for the covenant reversal that begins with the will. The phrase bĕkol lĕbābĕkā ûbĕkol napšĕkā (with all your heart and all your soul) echoes the Shema's demand-language (6:5), indicating that the return demanded is total reorientation, not grudging compliance.
3: וְשָׁב יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (wĕshāb Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā) — "and YHWH your God will return." The same verb shûb used of Israel in v2 is now used of YHWH — the divine turning mirrors and responds to the human turning. This verbal mirroring is theologically significant: YHWH's restoration is not independent of Israel's repentance but neither is it merely reactive; it is the covenantal counterpart to Israel's return. Wĕriḥămĕkā ("and will have compassion on you") — the noun raḥămîm (H7356, from the root for "womb") indicates the most intimate form of divine tenderness.
4: נִדַּח (niddaḥ, Niphal of H5080) — "driven out/banished." The passive form indicates that the scattering is not self-chosen exile but forced expulsion. The extremity of "the ends of heaven" (qĕṣēh hashshāmāyim) marks the reach of the divine retrieval: no geographic distance places the exiled Israelite beyond the covenant's reach. Wĕmishshām — "and from there YHWH your God will gather you."
5: הֵיטִיב (hêṭîb, Hiphil of H3190) — "to do good to, benefit": the restoration covenant promises material improvement. The phrase mēʾăbōtêkā ("more than your fathers") introduces a superlative: the restored community will exceed in blessing what any previous generation experienced. This "better than before" motif connects forward to the new covenant vision of Jer 31 and Ezek 36.
6: וּמָל יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֶת לְבָבְךָ (ûmāl Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾet lĕbābĕkā) — "and YHWH your God will circumcise your heart." The verb mûl (H4135) is the standard term for physical circumcision; applied to the heart, it describes the removal of the hardened outer covering that prevents full covenant loyalty. The decisive point is the subject: YHWH acts unilaterally. Unlike 10:16 where Israel is commanded "circumcise your heart," here YHWH performs the act himself. This is the closest Deuteronomy comes to announcing the new covenant — the heart-transformation that Jer 31:33 ("I will put my law on their heart") and Ezek 36:26 ("I will give you a new heart") will develop. Paul's argument in Rom 2:29 ("circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit") and Phil 3:3 draws directly on this trajectory.
7: נָתַן (nātan) — "will place/put": YHWH will place (yittēn) the curses upon the enemies. The covenant reversal is also judicial: those who afflicted Israel under divine sanction will themselves become the objects of covenant curse. This is the juridical dimension of restoration — not merely rescue but vindication.
8: וְאַתָּה תָשׁוּב (wĕʾattāh tāshûb) — "and you will return": the initial condition of v2 is now restated as a future certainty, not a hypothesis. The shift from conditional to indicative reflects the structure of grace: YHWH's prior action (v6, circumcision of heart) makes the human return possible and certain. Shāmaʿtā bĕqôl Yhwh — "you will obey the voice of YHWH" — the obedience follows from the heart-transformation, not the reverse.
9: יוֹתִירְךָ (yôtîrĕkā, Hiphil of H3498) — "will make you overflow/abound": the prosperity language echoes the blessing catalog of ch 28, but now in the register of restoration rather than initial covenant-entry. The three spheres of blessing — womb, animal stock, ground — are the fundamental productive capacities of agrarian life. Kî yāshûb Yhwh lāśôś ʿālêkā lĕṭôb kaʾăsher śās ʿal ʾăbōtêkā: "as he rejoiced over your fathers" — YHWH rejoices (śôś) over the restored people, not merely restoring a legal status but expressing divine delight.
10: בְּשׁוּבְךָ (bĕshûbĕkā) — "when you return": the absolute form of the condition — with all your heart (lēbāb) and all your soul (nepeš) — recapitulates the Shema's demand. The restoration covenant is not a lowering of the standard; it is the same total-person covenant loyalty, now made possible by the circumcision of v6. Bĕkol lĕbābĕkā ûbĕkol napšĕkā here serves as the closing bracket of the section that opened at v2 with the same phrase.
11: לֹא נִפְלֵאת (lōʾ niphlĕʾt, Niphal of H6381) — "not too wonderful/extraordinary." The root plʾ means extraordinary, beyond ordinary capacity — used of divine wonders (Exod 3:20) and things too great for human comprehension. The denial — "this commandment is not too extraordinary for you, not too far away" — counters the defeatist assumption that covenant faithfulness requires superhuman capacity. The NT picks this up; Paul quotes vv12-14 in Rom 10:6-8 to argue that Christ is not inaccessibly remote.
12: לֹא בַשָּׁמַיִם הִוא (lōʾ bāshāmayim hîʾ) — "not in heaven is it." The rhetorical question "who will go up to heaven for us and get it for us?" frames the alternative as an impossibility — no one is capable of the ascent required. In its Deuteronomic context, this means: the Torah does not require superhuman effort; it has been given accessibly. Paul re-reads the rhetorical question in Rom 10:6-8: "who will ascend into heaven?" (= to bring Christ down) and "who will descend into the abyss?" (= to bring Christ up from the dead) — he applies the Deuteronomic impossibility-argument typologically to show that faith does not require impossible acts because Christ has already accomplished the ascent and descent.
13: מֵעֵבֶר לַיָּם (mēʿēber layyām) — "beyond the sea." The paired impossibilities — heaven (v12) and sea (v13) — represent the two axes of inaccessibility in ancient cosmology: vertical and horizontal. The sea (yām) often carries the connotation of the deep and chaotic in Hebrew cosmology. Paul reads this as "the abyss" (ābyssos) in his re-application (Rom 10:7). The point in both contexts is the same: the saving word is not inaccessible by cosmic distance.
14: כִּי קָרוֹב אֵלֶיךָ הַדָּבָר מְאֹד (kî qārôb ʾēlêkā haddābār mĕʾōd) — "but the word is very near to you." Qārôb (H7138, near, close) is the climactic reversal of the two impossibility-scenarios. The three locations — mouth (bĕpîkā), heart (bĕlibĕkā), and the stated purpose "to do it" (laʿăśôtô) — describe a word that has already been internalized and is ready for enactment. The triad implies: oral recitation (in your mouth), inner comprehension (in your heart), and active obedience (to do it). Paul quotes this verse as the starting point for his argument that "the word of faith" (the gospel) is equally accessible — the Torah's claim for the nearness of the covenant word becomes the gospel's claim for the nearness of Christ.
16: לְאַהֲבָה אֶת יְהוָה (lĕʾahăbāh ʾet Yhwh) — "to love YHWH": the imperative returns to the ground-level command. Obedience and love are not presented as alternatives; the verb ʾāhab in covenant context is the encompassing term for covenantal loyalty-in-action. The three-fold structure — love YHWH, walk in his ways, keep his commandments — recapitulates the Shema's demand in its fullest form. The result: blessing and multiplication in the land.
17: יִפְנֶה לְבָבְךָ (yipneh lĕbābĕkā) — "if your heart turns away": the verb pānāh (H6437) here has the sense of deflection — the heart that was to be wholly toward YHWH (6:5) turning to face something else. The consequence — serving other gods — follows naturally; the heart's orientation determines the object of worship. The "turning" of the heart is the internal act that precedes all external apostasy.
18: אָבֹד תֹּאבֵדוּן (ābōd tōʾbēdûn) — infinitive absolute intensifying the finite verb: "you will certainly/utterly perish." The intensified form marks the severity; this is not a vague threat but a covenantal certainty. Lōʾ taʾărîkûn yāmîm ʿal hāʾădāmāh — "you will not prolong your days on the land": longevity in the land, promised for obedience (5:16; 6:2), is here reversed as the sanction for covenant defection.
19: הַעִידֹתִי בָכֶם הַיּוֹם אֶת הַשָּׁמַיִם וְאֶת הָאָרֶץ (haʿîdōtî bākem hayyôm ʾet hashshāmayim wĕʾet hāʾāreṣ) — "I call to witness against you today heaven and earth." The formal invocation of cosmic witnesses is the standard closing of a vassal-treaty — the suzerain calls on the divine witnesses to guarantee the covenant terms. Since YHWH himself is the suzerain, the witnesses are the impersonal creation: heaven and earth. The formal verb ʿûd (H5749, Hiphil: to bear witness/testify against) gives this a legal register: Moses is making a formal treaty-deposition. Habĕḥer baḥayyîm lĕmaʿan tiḥyeh — "choose life so that you may live" — the imperative plus result clause is the clearest single statement of Deuteronomy's entire argument.
20: לְאַהֲבָה אֶת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (lĕʾahăbāh ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā) — "to love YHWH your God": the closing verse of the section recapitulates its three-term structure: love (ʾāhab), obey (shāmaʿ bĕqôlô), hold fast (dābaq bô, H1692 — to cling to, as the vine to the trellis, as husband and wife, Gen 2:24). כִּי הוּא חַיֶּיךָ (kî hûʾ ḥayyêkā) — "for he is your life": the capstone of the whole ethical argument. YHWH is not merely the source of life as a secondary cause; he is constitutively Israel's life. Obedience is not the mechanism for securing life from YHWH; it is the way of inhabiting the life that is already YHWH himself.
